---
title: "Exploring Best Practices for Agentic Coding: A Deep Dive Through the Lens of Claude Code"
author:
  - name: Chris Endemann
    email: endemann@wisc.edu
date: 2026-03-09
date-format: long
image: "../../../images/agentic-coding.png"
categories:
  - Guides
  - GenAI
  - LLM
  - Agentic coding
  - Software development
  - Best practices
  - Security
---

AI coding agents — tools that can autonomously write, edit, run, and test code on your behalf — are rapidly changing how software gets built. The space is crowded and evolving fast: [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview), [GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot), [Cursor](https://www.cursor.com/), [Windsurf](https://windsurf.com/), [Augment Code](https://www.augmentcode.com/), [Amazon Q Developer](https://aws.amazon.com/q/developer/), [Gemini Code Assist](https://cloud.google.com/products/gemini-code-assist), and [GitLab Duo](https://about.gitlab.com/gitlab-duo/) are among the most prominent, with new entrants appearing regularly.

Best practices in this space are still being discovered — by the ML+X community and the broader developer ecosystem alike. This guide is our attempt to start mapping what works, using **[Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/)** as the primary lens. Claude Code is Anthropic's agentic coding tool — distinct from the [Claude.ai](https://claude.ai) chat interface — and it comes in several forms: a **CLI**, a **desktop app**, and a **web IDE** at [claude.ai/code](https://claude.ai/code). All give the agent real shell access to read, write, and execute code, which makes it a great lens for exploring the trade-offs of agentic coding: permissions, context management, and cost. We'll reference Claude.ai, GitHub Copilot, and other tools for comparison where useful.

This is a first pass based on early experience — we expect it to evolve as the ML+X community builds more hands-on knowledge. If you have tips, corrections, or experiences to share, please leave a comment below or start a discussion on the [Nexus Q&A](https://github.com/UW-Madison-DataScience/ML-X-Nexus/discussions/categories/q-a).

## What is agentic coding?

Traditional AI code assistants (like early GitHub Copilot or ChatGPT) work in a simple loop: you ask, they suggest, you accept or reject. **Agentic** coding tools go further. They can:

- Read and navigate your entire codebase
- Execute shell commands and run tests
- Edit multiple files in a single pass
- Iterate on their own output (fix errors, re-run tests, refine)
- Operate semi-autonomously over multi-step tasks

This is powerful, but it also means these tools have real access to your system — and the potential to do real damage if not managed carefully.

## The landscape at a glance

Before diving into Claude Code specifically, here's a rough map of the major agentic coding tools as of early 2026:

| Tool | Interface | Agentic? | Cost model | Notable strengths |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Claude Code** | CLI, desktop app, [web IDE](https://claude.ai/code) | Yes — full shell access | Pay-per-token (API) or Max plan | Deep reasoning, explicit permission model, CLAUDE.md |
| **GitHub Copilot** | VS Code/IDE, GitHub.com | Yes — agent mode + async coding agent | Subscription + usage-based | Tight GitHub integration, async PR creation, wide model selection |
| **Cursor** | Custom IDE (VS Code fork) | Yes | Subscription | Best-in-class IDE UX, fast inline edits, multi-file context |
| **Windsurf** | Custom IDE | Yes | Subscription (free tier available) | Good value, smooth agentic flow |
| **Augment Code** | IDE extension | Yes | Subscription | Large context window, codebase-aware |
| **Amazon Q Developer** | IDE, CLI, AWS console | Yes | Free tier / Pro | Deep AWS integration |
| **Gemini Code Assist** | IDE, Google Cloud | Yes | Free tier / Enterprise | Google Cloud integration |
| **GitLab Duo** | GitLab IDE, MR workflows | Yes | GitLab subscription add-on | Native GitLab CI/CD integration |

This space is moving fast — capabilities and pricing change frequently. See [Coding Agents Comparison](https://artificialanalysis.ai/insights/coding-agents-comparison) for up-to-date benchmarks and pricing.

In practice, many developers use **multiple tools**: a chat UI for brainstorming and review, an agentic tool for multi-step feature work, and an IDE copilot for inline completions throughout the day.

### What the same task looks like across different tools

To make these distinctions concrete, let's walk through the same scenario — *"I have a repo on GitHub and I want Claude to add a utility function, write tests, and open a PR"* — across Claude.ai, both forms of Claude Code, and GitHub Copilot.

#### Claude.ai (chat — not Claude Code)

[Claude.ai](https://claude.ai) is Anthropic's general-purpose chat interface. It's **not** an agentic coding tool — it can't execute code, edit files, or run commands on your system. It has no access to your codebase; you provide context by copying and pasting code snippets into the conversation.

1. Start a new conversation at [claude.ai](https://claude.ai)
2. Paste in the relevant code (e.g., the contents of `src/utils/` and a few example utilities)
3. Ask: *"Add a `slugify` function that matches the style of these existing utilities. Also write tests."*
4. Claude generates the code and tests in the chat
5. **You** copy the output back into your editor, create a branch, commit, and open the PR yourself

**Friction:** You're the middleware in both directions — pasting code in and copying code out. But notice what's *not* here: permission prompts, approve/deny flows, or any risk of it running a bad command. Claude.ai can't touch your system, so the conversation feels fast and fluid even though you do all the manual work.

**Best for:** Quick code generation, architecture discussions, explaining unfamiliar code, and brainstorming — any task where you're happy to provide context manually and apply changes yourself.

#### Claude Code (CLI)

```bash
# Clone and navigate to your repo
git clone git@github.com:yourorg/yourrepo.git && cd yourrepo

# Start Claude Code
claude

# Now you're in an interactive session. Type your request:
> Look at src/utils/ and add a slugify function that matches the style
> of existing utilities. Write tests too. Create a branch, commit, and
> open a PR when you're done.
```

Claude Code will:

1. Read your existing utils to understand the style
2. **Ask permission** to create a new file → you approve (or it auto-approves if you've configured that)
3. Write the function
4. **Ask permission** to create the test file → you approve
5. Write tests
6. **Ask permission** to run the tests → you approve
7. Run `pytest` (or whatever your test runner is), see results
8. If tests fail, iterate — fix the code, re-run
9. **Ask permission** to create a branch and commit → you approve
10. **Ask permission** to push and open a PR → you approve

**Friction:** The permission prompts keep you in the loop but slow things down. You can reduce this by configuring auto-approve for low-risk actions (file reads, test runs) in your settings or `CLAUDE.md`. The sweet spot is usually: auto-approve reads and test execution, manually approve writes and git operations.

**Best for:** Multi-step feature development, large refactors, debugging complex issues — any task that benefits from the agent being able to run and test its own code.

#### Claude Code (Browser IDE at [claude.ai/code](https://claude.ai/code))

Claude Code also runs as a **browser-based IDE** at [claude.ai/code](https://claude.ai/code) — you get the same agentic capabilities as the CLI without installing anything locally. Don't confuse this with the regular [claude.ai](https://claude.ai) chat: `claude.ai/code` gives you a full development environment with shell access, file editing, and the same permission model as the CLI.

The workflow is essentially the same as the CLI version above, but:

- No local setup — useful for quick starts or when you're not on your main machine
- The remote environment is sandboxed by default, reducing risk to your local system
- May have resource limits (compute, storage, session duration) compared to running locally

**Best for:** Getting started with agentic coding, working from machines where you can't install CLI tools, team demos, and sandboxed experimentation.

#### GitHub Copilot (Agent mode in VS Code)

1. Open your repo in VS Code with the Copilot extension installed
2. Open the Copilot chat panel (Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+I)
3. Select agent mode, choose Claude as the model
4. Type: *"Add a slugify function to src/utils/ matching the existing style. Write tests."*

Copilot will:

1. Read relevant files
2. Create/edit files directly — **no permission prompt by default** in many configurations
3. May run tests if it decides to (or you can ask it to)
4. You review the changes in VS Code's diff view
5. **You** handle the git workflow (branch, commit, push, PR) — or use the Copilot coding agent for that

**Friction:** The IDE experience is smooth but you have less visibility into *why* the agent made certain choices. Agent mode is also still evolving — for complex multi-step tasks it may not iterate as effectively as Claude Code's loop. The upside is zero context-switching: you're already in your editor.

#### GitHub Copilot (Coding agent — async)

1. Go to your repo on GitHub.com
2. Create an issue: *"Add a slugify utility function to src/utils/ with tests"*
3. Assign the issue to Copilot (or comment `@copilot` on the issue)
4. Walk away — Copilot works in a cloud environment

Copilot will:

1. Create a branch
2. Implement the function and tests
3. Open a PR referencing the issue
4. You get a notification when the PR is ready to review

**Friction:** This is the most hands-off option, but you have the least control during execution. Works best for well-scoped, clearly described issues. If the task is ambiguous or requires judgment calls, you may end up doing multiple rounds of PR review and comments to guide it.

#### Key takeaway

The same task ranges from fully manual (Claude.ai — you apply every change) to fully hands-off (Copilot coding agent — you just review the PR). But "more autonomous" doesn't always mean "smoother experience."

Counterintuitively, **Claude.ai can feel lower-friction than Claude Code** for many tasks — the chat interface just *answers*, with no permission prompts or approve/deny flow. You lose the ability to have Claude execute things directly, but you gain a frictionless conversation. Claude Code (CLI, desktop app, or web) is far more capable — it can run tests, iterate on failures, and push code — but its default guardrails (which exist for good reason) mean more interruptions until you tune them.

The trade-off is between **autonomy** and **control**:

- **Claude.ai (chat)** — not agentic, but fluid and zero-risk. You do the manual work.
- **Claude Code (CLI, desktop, or web)** — fully agentic, with an explicit permission model you can tune from conservative to autonomous.
- **Copilot agent mode (IDE)** — agentic within the IDE, fewer interruptions than Claude Code, but less granular control.
- **Copilot coding agent (async)** — most hands-off, but least control during execution.

Pick based on the task. Sensitive work or unfamiliar codebase? Claude Code's guardrails are a feature. Quick question about your repo? Claude.ai with a connected repo is hard to beat. Cranking through well-understood changes? Copilot's flow might be preferable.

## Working effectively with Claude Code

This is the core of the guide. Whether you're using the CLI, desktop app, or web IDE, these practices apply across all Claude Code surfaces.

### Think in features, not projects

One of the biggest lessons from working with agentic coding tools: **use them for feature-level development, not for building entire projects in one shot.**

Why? Because agents work best with clear, well-scoped requests. The less clarity you provide, the more the agent has to guess — and guessing leads to:

- Agentic loops (trying approaches, failing, trying again)
- Drift from your intended architecture
- Wasted tokens and time
- Code that technically works but doesn't match your vision

**Precise requests get precise results.** Instead of "build me a web app with auth," try:

- "Add a login form component that submits to `/api/auth/login` and stores the JWT in a httpOnly cookie"
- "Write a pytest fixture that creates a test database with the schema from `models.py`"
- "Refactor the `process_data` function in `pipeline.py` to handle the case where `input_df` has missing columns"

Each of these is a single, well-defined task that an agent can execute without ambiguity.

### Use `CLAUDE.md` as your control surface

Claude Code reads a `CLAUDE.md` file in your project root for persistent instructions. This is one of the most underrated features — it's your main lever for shaping how the agent behaves across sessions.

**Prevent runaway loops:**
```markdown
## Testing requirements
- Always run the full test suite (`pytest tests/`) after making changes
- If tests fail, fix the failing tests before moving on
- Do not push code with failing tests
```

This single instruction saves enormous headaches. Without it, the agent might push broken code, you discover test failures in CI, and then you're spending time fixing things that should have been caught locally.

**Enforce project conventions:**
```markdown
## Code style
- Use type hints for all function signatures
- Follow the existing import ordering convention
- Do not add new dependencies without asking first
```

**Limit destructive actions:**
```markdown
## Safety
- Never run `rm -rf` on any directory
- Never force-push to any branch
- Never modify files in the `config/production/` directory
- Always create a new branch for changes; never commit directly to main
```

**Provide architectural context:**
```markdown
## Project structure
- API routes go in `src/routes/`
- Business logic goes in `src/services/`
- Database models are in `src/models/`
- Tests mirror the source structure under `tests/`
```

The more context you provide in `CLAUDE.md`, the fewer agentic loops the agent needs to figure out your project.

### Tune the permission dial

Claude Code's permission system is the main thing that distinguishes it from tools that "just go." By default, it asks before every file write, shell command, and git operation. This is safe but slow.

The key insight: **permissions aren't all-or-nothing**. You can configure a spectrum:

- **Start conservative** — approve everything manually while you're learning what the agent does
- **Auto-approve low-risk actions** — file reads, grep/search, test execution. These rarely cause harm and the prompts add friction without adding safety.
- **Manually approve writes and git operations** — this is where real damage can happen (overwriting files, force-pushing, committing secrets)
- **Use `CLAUDE.md` safety rules** as a second layer — even if you auto-approve shell commands, the agent will respect instructions like "never force-push"

The sweet spot for most developers: auto-approve reads and test runs, manually approve everything else. As you build trust with specific workflows, you can loosen further.

### Commit early, commit often

When working with an agentic tool:

1. **Commit before starting** — create a clean baseline you can revert to
2. **Commit after each successful change** — don't let the agent build up a massive diff
3. **Use branches** — never let an agent work directly on `main`
4. **Review diffs before pushing** — the agent writes the code, but you own it

### Review everything

Just because an agent wrote the code doesn't mean it's correct, secure, or maintainable. Treat agent-generated code with the same scrutiny you'd apply to any pull request:

- Read the diffs
- Run the tests
- Check for security issues (hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, etc.)
- Verify it matches your architectural patterns
- Make sure it doesn't introduce unnecessary complexity

## Managing costs

Agentic coding tools that use API tokens (like Claude Code) charge per token, and agentic workflows are token-hungry — the agent reads files, reasons through problems, writes code, runs commands, reads output, and iterates. A single focused task might use 50K–200K tokens. A sprawling, underspecified session can easily burn through 1M+ tokens.

### What does this actually cost?

Rough estimates for Claude Code usage (as of early 2026, using Claude Sonnet as the default model):

| Usage pattern | Tokens/day (est.) | Daily cost (est.) |
|---|---|---|
| Light — a few focused tasks | 100K–300K | $0.50–$2 |
| Moderate — active feature development | 500K–1.5M | $3–$10 |
| Heavy — large refactors, multi-step debugging | 2M–5M+ | $15–$40+ |

These are rough ranges and will vary based on model choice (Opus costs more than Sonnet, Haiku costs less), codebase size (larger context = more input tokens), and how many agentic loops occur. Anthropic also offers a **Max plan** with higher rate limits for teams doing heavy agentic work.

For comparison, GitHub Copilot subscriptions run $10–$39/month depending on tier, with usage-based pricing for premium models beyond included allowances.

### Strategies to keep costs down

- **Be specific in your prompts** — vague requests lead to more agentic loops, which means more tokens. "Add a login form" costs more than "Add a React component at `src/components/LoginForm.tsx` that posts email/password to `/api/auth/login`"
- **Use the right tier of model for the task** — use Haiku or Sonnet for straightforward tasks, reserve Opus for complex reasoning
- **Break large tasks into smaller ones** — each focused session is cheaper than one sprawling conversation that loses context and re-reads files
- **Use `CLAUDE.md`** to provide project context upfront — this reduces the amount of exploration the agent needs to do
- **Set spending alerts and hard limits** on your Anthropic API account
- **Monitor session token usage** — Claude Code shows token counts in the session, so you can see when things are getting expensive and redirect

### Energy and environmental considerations

It's worth acknowledging that agentic coding is more compute-intensive than traditional code completion. Each agentic loop involves multiple LLM inference calls, each of which consumes GPU compute (and the energy that powers it). A heavy day of agentic coding might involve hundreds of inference calls.

The environmental cost of AI inference is an active area of research and debate. Some perspective:

- A single LLM inference call uses roughly 0.001–0.01 kWh of energy (varies widely by model size and provider infrastructure)
- A heavy day of agentic coding (thousands of calls) might use 1–10 kWh — comparable to running a desktop computer for a day
- Cloud providers are increasingly powering data centers with renewable energy, but this varies by region and provider

This doesn't mean you shouldn't use agentic tools — the productivity gains can be substantial. But it's a reason to be intentional: don't let an agent spin in wasteful loops when a well-scoped prompt would get the job done in one pass. **Efficient prompting is both cheaper and greener.**

## Security fundamentals

When you give an AI agent access to your terminal, you're trusting it with everything your shell user can do. Here's how to limit the blast radius.

### Scope your credentials

The agent can read environment variables, inspect dotfiles, and run commands like `cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa` or `echo $GITHUB_TOKEN`. You need to make credentials **structurally inaccessible**, not just hidden.

- **Create dedicated, minimal-scope tokens** — a fine-grained GitHub token scoped to only the repos and permissions the agent needs. If it only needs to push to one repo, don't give it access to your entire GitHub account.
- **Use a bot account** for agent-driven git operations rather than your personal credentials
- **Never reuse your daily-driver SSH keys** in an agentic environment — generate dedicated deploy keys
- **Don't pass secrets as environment variables** in containers — use Docker secrets, mount a read-only credential socket, or configure a git credential helper on the host
- **Set spending limits** on API keys and use separate keys from your personal/production ones
- Add `.env`, `credentials.json`, `*.pem`, `*.key`, `.netrc` to `.gitignore` **before the agent ever runs**

### Sandbox when possible

Running an agent on your bare metal dev machine means it has access to everything you do. **Any sandboxing is better than none.**

| Scenario | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| **Solo dev, familiar with Docker** | Standard container with a mounted project directory — gives you full control |
| **Team environment, need consistency** | Purpose-built sandbox (Anthropic's sandboxed containers, E2B) — reduces setup burden |
| **CI/CD pipelines** | Ephemeral containers destroyed after each run — the safest option at scale |
| **Working with sensitive data** | Purpose-built sandbox with network restrictions — limits exfiltration risk |
| **Quick local experimentation** | Even a basic container is better than bare metal |

A standard Docker container gets you 80% of the way there — the agent can't access your host filesystem outside the mounted volume. Purpose-built sandboxes close the remaining gaps around network isolation, filesystem snapshotting, and security hardening. See [Anthropic's sandboxing documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing) and their [engineering deep-dive](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-sandboxing) for details.

### Watch for prompt injection and runaway agents

- Be careful with files that contain instructions or prompts — an agent reading a malicious `README.md` or issue body could be tricked into harmful actions
- **Never** give an agent unsupervised access to production systems, databases, or deployment pipelines
- Set up **git hooks** or CI checks as safety nets (e.g., prevent force-pushes to `main`)
- Keep your work committed frequently so you can recover from mistakes
- Set **spending limits** and don't hesitate to interrupt (`Ctrl+C`) and redirect when the agent is looping

## Platform and deployment notes

### CI/CD and remote deployment

Agentic coding tools can run beyond your local machine:

- **Cloud VMs and remote servers** — run Claude Code over SSH. Your local system stays untouched, and you can use more powerful hardware.
- **CI/CD pipelines** — GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and similar systems can invoke Claude Code or Copilot coding agent in **ephemeral environments** that are destroyed after each run. This is the safest way to use agentic tools at scale.
- **Sandboxed containers** — Anthropic offers purpose-built sandboxed environments for running Claude Code in isolated contexts. Third-party providers like E2B offer similar capabilities.

The key insight: **the more system access a tool has, the more carefully you need to manage it**.

### A note for GitLab users

Many teams — including many at UW-Madison — use GitLab rather than GitHub. Claude Code works with GitLab, but the integration is **less mature** than the GitHub experience.

**What works well:**

- **Claude Code CLI with GitLab repos** — the core experience (reading code, editing files, running commands) works identically regardless of your git host. Claude Code operates on your local checkout, so the remote platform doesn't matter for day-to-day coding.
- **GitLab CI/CD integration** — Anthropic provides [official documentation for running Claude Code in GitLab CI/CD pipelines](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/gitlab-ci-cd), including merge request review and test scaffolding.
- **Git operations** — push, pull, branching, and committing all work normally since these are standard git operations.

**What's different or limited compared to GitHub:**

- **No native GitLab integration in Claude Code's Slack bot** — the Slack integration currently only supports GitHub repos. GitLab support is [an open feature request](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/21527).
- **No `@claude` mention in GitLab issues/MRs** — GitHub Copilot's coding agent lets you assign issues to Copilot or mention it in PRs. There's no equivalent native integration for GitLab yet, though [GitLab is working on it](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/issues/557820).
- **Community-built CI/CD tooling** — while official docs exist, you may find yourself using [community solutions](https://github.com/RealMikeChong/claude-code-for-gitlab) to replicate the smoother GitHub Actions experience.
- **Self-hosted GitLab** — if your institution runs a self-hosted GitLab instance, be aware that Claude Code sends code context to Anthropic's API for processing. This may raise compliance concerns depending on your institution's data policies.

**Practical advice:** The CLI workflow is essentially identical — focus your setup effort on CI/CD integration. For MR review automation, use Claude Code in your `.gitlab-ci.yml` with the `claude -p` (prompt) flag for non-interactive pipeline usage. If your institution has data sensitivity requirements, check with your IT governance team before sending code to external APIs — this applies to **all** cloud-based AI coding tools, not just Claude.

## Summary

Agentic coding tools are genuinely powerful — they can dramatically accelerate feature development, help you explore unfamiliar codebases, and automate tedious multi-step tasks. But they require a different mindset than traditional code assistants:

1. **Scope your requests tightly** — features, not projects
2. **Use `CLAUDE.md`** to encode guardrails and project context
3. **Tune permissions deliberately** — start conservative, loosen as you build trust
4. **Protect your credentials** — fine-grained tokens, structural isolation, `.gitignore`
5. **Sandbox when possible** — containers, VMs, ephemeral CI environments
6. **Monitor costs** — set limits, be specific, use the right model for the task
7. **Commit frequently** — keep escape hatches available
8. **Review everything** — you're the engineer; the agent is a very fast intern

The technology is moving fast, and best practices will continue to evolve. The core principle stays the same: **give agents the minimum access they need, provide maximum clarity in your instructions, and always keep a human in the loop for decisions that matter**.

## Further reading and perspectives

Agentic coding is evolving fast. Here are some of the best resources for staying current:

### Official documentation and guides

- [Best Practices for Claude Code](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices) — Anthropic's official guide, covering context management, multi-session patterns, CLAUDE.md, and batch operations
- [Claude Code Sandboxing Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing) — reference for configuring Claude Code's built-in sandboxing, including OS-level primitives (Linux bubblewrap, macOS Seatbelt) and deny rules for sensitive files
- [Making Claude Code More Secure and Autonomous](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-sandboxing) — Anthropic Engineering's deep-dive into their dual-layer sandboxing architecture (filesystem + network isolation)
- [Mitigating the Risk of Prompt Injections](https://www.anthropic.com/research/prompt-injection-defenses) — Anthropic Research on defending AI agents against prompt injection, including their use of reinforcement learning to build injection robustness into Claude
- [GitHub Copilot: Meet the New Coding Agent](https://github.blog/news-insights/product-news/github-copilot-meet-the-new-coding-agent/) — GitHub's announcement of their enterprise-ready coding agent that spins up secure environments via GitHub Actions
- [GitHub Copilot Coding Agent 101](https://github.blog/ai-and-ml/github-copilot/github-copilot-coding-agent-101-getting-started-with-agentic-workflows-on-github/) — GitHub's getting-started guide for agentic workflows, including environment setup and PR creation
- [What's New with GitHub Copilot Coding Agent](https://github.blog/ai-and-ml/github-copilot/whats-new-with-github-copilot-coding-agent/) — latest updates including self-review, security scanning, and custom agents

### Community voices and analysis

- [Agentic Engineering Patterns](https://simonwillison.net/2026/Feb/23/agentic-engineering-patterns/) — Simon Willison's guide to coding practices for getting the best results from agents like Claude Code and Codex. He frames this as "expertise amplification, not expertise replacement"
- [A Guide to Which AI to Use in the Agentic Era](https://www.oneusefulthing.org/p/a-guide-to-which-ai-to-use-in-the) — Ethan Mollick's updated guide arguing that "using AI" now means agents with tools, not chatbots, and that users must think in terms of Models, Apps, and Harnesses
- [How I Use Claude Code (+ My Best Tips)](https://www.builder.io/blog/claude-code) — practical walkthrough from Builder.io on real-world Claude Code workflows
- [The Complete Guide to Agentic Coding in 2026](https://www.teamday.ai/blog/complete-guide-agentic-coding-2026) — broad overview comparing tools, workflows, and team strategies
- [Redefining the Software Engineering Profession for AI](https://cacm.acm.org/opinion/redefining-the-software-engineering-profession-for-ai/) — ACM opinion piece on how AI amplifies senior talent but risks leaving junior developers without the chance to develop architectural intuition

### Tool comparisons

- [Cursor vs Windsurf vs Claude Code in 2026](https://dev.to/pockit_tools/cursor-vs-windsurf-vs-claude-code-in-2026-the-honest-comparison-after-using-all-three-3gof) — hands-on comparison arguing Cursor has the best IDE UX, Claude Code leads on deep reasoning and terminal-first workflows, and Windsurf offers the best value
- [Coding Agents Comparison: Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, and More](https://artificialanalysis.ai/insights/coding-agents-comparison) — benchmark-driven comparison with pricing breakdowns and SWE-bench scores

### Security

- [AI Coding Tools Exploded in 2025. The First Security Exploits Show What Could Go Wrong](https://fortune.com/2025/12/15/ai-coding-tools-security-exploit-software/) — Fortune's reporting on the "IDEsaster" vulnerabilities found across Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, and other tools
- [Researcher Uncovers 30+ Flaws in AI Coding Tools](https://thehackernews.com/2025/12/researchers-uncover-30-flaws-in-ai.html) — technical breakdown of the universal attack chains affecting major AI IDEs
- [Security Flaws in Claude Code Risk Stolen Data, System Takeover](https://devops.com/security-flaws-in-anthropics-claude-code-risk-stolen-data-system-takeover/) — Check Point's findings on Claude Code-specific CVEs, including hook injection and API key theft
- [AI Agent Security Risks in 2026: A Practitioner's Guide](https://blog.cyberdesserts.com/ai-agent-security-risks/) — practical guide to defending against prompt injection, credential theft, and MCP vulnerabilities

This is an area where best practices are being written in real time. What works today may be outdated in six months. Stay plugged into the communities above, and don't assume any single tool or configuration is permanently "safe."

## Questions or feedback?
Have thoughts, corrections, or tips from your own experience? Leave a comment below — we'd love to hear what's working (or not) for you. You can also post to the [Nexus Q&A](https://github.com/UW-Madison-DataScience/ML-X-Nexus/discussions/categories/q-a) on GitHub for longer discussions.
